name: Inner-City Muslim Action Network
mission: IMAN incorporated as a nonprofit in 1997 through the drive of people directly affected by and deeply invested in social issues affecting communities of color living on Chicago’s South Side. Since that time, IMAN has steadily grown and, in 2016, opened a second office in Atlanta to continue mobilizing a cross-section of people committed to this mission.
theoryOfChange: IMAN uses a holistic, integrative approach combining direct services (health, housing) with systemic change (organizing, advocacy) and cultural work (arts) to address structural injustices.
scoreSummary: INNER-CITY MUSLIM ACTION NETWORK shows good alignment and limited impact, with zakat compliance (minor risk deductions applied).

evaluationSources: MacArthur Fellowship (awarded to Executive Director Rami Nashashibi for work with IMAN) (MacArthur Foundation)
Award for Community Health Center upgrades (U.S. Department of Health and Human Services)
Leaders for a New Chicago Award (awarded to Chief Executive Director Alia J. Bilal for work with IMAN) (The Field Foundation in partnership with MacArthur)

judge_issues.message: The rationale does not adequately address the significant risk factors, particularly the high CEO compensation (15.1% of revenue) and the high fundraising costs (88% of total expenses). These are flagged as 'high' severity risks and should be explicitly mentioned in the rationale to justify the risk deduction.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: IMAN demonstrates strong financial stability and fundraising efficiency, though its overall score is moderated by a program expense ratio of 65.9% and a need for more robust impact data.
areas_for_improvement: program expense ratio of 65.9% is lower than the industry benchmark for high-performing nonprofits

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization shows medium alignment for Muslim donors by offering Zakat-eligible giving options that support specific asnaf categories through domestic poverty relief [2][3]. Its focus on community organizing and returning citizens addresses urgent social needs in urban centers [4].
dimension_explanations.impact: IMAN operates as a direct service provider, managing a total revenue of $18,475,567 to fund its health and reentry initiatives [5]. While the organization maintains a healthy 9.9 months of working capital, its impact score reflects a moderate program expense ratio of 65.9% and a lack of publicly available cost-per-beneficiary metrics [1][5].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) fosters health, wellness, and healing in Chicago and Atlanta through integrated social services and community organizing.
strengths: High fundraising efficiency with a cost of only $0.02 per $1 raised
Strong liquidity with 9.9 months of working capital available
Direct service model providing tangible housing and health resources
baseline_narrative.summary: IMAN provides transitional housing, life skills education, and sustainable construction training for returning citizens while operating a community health center and green reentry programs [1][5]. The organization is Zakat-eligible, allowing donors to contribute their obligatory alms toward programs that support marginalized populations [2].

rich_narrative.headline: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) fosters health, wellness, and healing in Chicago and Atlanta through integrated social services and community organizing.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who prioritize domestic social justice and want to support holistic reentry services for formerly incarcerated Muslims in major US urban centers.
donor_motivations: Want to fulfill zakat through domestic poverty alleviation
Value long-term community presence and institutional stability
Interested in the intersection of faith, health, and environmental justice
Support direct service models for marginalized urban populations
giving_considerations: High executive pay may be a deterrent for efficiency-minded donors
Program expense ratio is lower than many international relief peers
Focus is localized to specific urban hubs rather than national reach
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking high-efficiency international relief or those who require perfect scores on third-party accountability benchmarks like the BBB.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: While the organization has a clear strategy and significant awards, it lacks publicly available, rigorous third-party evaluations of its long-term outcomes.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: IMAN utilizes an integrated approach that combines health services, transitional housing, and vocational training to break cycles of poverty and recidivism.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that the 'Green ReEntry' model effectively reduces recidivism compared to standard programs.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: Despite significant assets, the recent decline in revenue and high operating costs for health and housing suggest a strong need for new capital.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="2">integrated 'Green ReEntry' and health center model</cite> which is unique in the domestic Muslim nonprofit space.

similar_organizations.differentiator: Broader national network of food pantries and shelters

similar_organizations.differentiator: Higher program expense ratio and international focus

strengths.detail: IMAN demonstrates high fiscal discipline in its development efforts, spending just <cite id="6">$0.02 per $1 raised</cite>, which is significantly better than industry averages.
The organization maintains <cite id="9">9.9 months of working capital</cite>, providing a strong safety net for its <cite id="2">transitional housing and health programs</cite>.
Operating for <cite id="1">nearly 30 years</cite>, IMAN has established deep roots in <cite id="10">Chicago and Atlanta</cite> through its integrated service model.

strengths_deep_dive: IMAN's operational strength lies in its ability to leverage <cite id="4">significant community contributions</cite> into high-impact direct services. By maintaining <cite id="11">100% independent board members</cite>, the organization ensures a level of objective oversight. Furthermore, its <cite id="12">documented theory of change</cite> and recognition through <cite id="13">multiple MacArthur Foundation grants</cite> underscore its reputation as a leading voice in urban Muslim community development.
rich_narrative.summary: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) is a community-based organization that has operated since <cite id="1">its founding in 1997</cite> to provide integrated social services in Chicago and Atlanta. The organization manages a diverse portfolio of programs including a <cite id="2">community health center, green reentry initiatives, and transitional housing</cite> for formerly incarcerated individuals. In the 2023 fiscal year, IMAN reported <cite id="3">total revenue of $15,923,414</cite>, primarily driven by <cite id="4">$12,509,163 in contributions</cite> and nearly $3 million in program service revenue. While the organization maintains a <cite id="5">program expense ratio of 65.9%</cite>, it distinguishes itself through exceptional fundraising efficiency, spending only <cite id="6">$0.03 to raise every $1</cite> in charitable support. IMAN's model is deeply rooted in spiritual and social justice frameworks, offering <cite id="7">zakat-eligible giving opportunities</cite> that serve the poor, the needy, and those in debt or transition. Despite a <cite id="8">3-year revenue CAGR of -21.1%</cite>, the organization remains financially resilient with <cite id="9">9.9 months of working capital</cite> available to sustain its direct service operations during economic fluctuations.
